title: Segregation of duties — toxic role combinations in IAM
exclusions: Does NOT assess business-justified exceptions; captured in a lookup.
evidence: Daily 'sod_toxic_combos' writes toxic-combo findings to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: SOX SoD — matching toxic combinations is the canonical SOX-ITGC test.
PCI 7.2 privilege assignment — SoD is a typical implementation.
A.5.3 segregation of duties — explicit policy control.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When any principal (human or service account) holds two roles listed as a toxic combo in sod_matrix.csv, the UC fires with principal, role_a, role_b.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Principals with role-combos that are expressly marked permitted_with_compensating=true do NOT fire.

spl:
| rest /services/iam/principals splunk_server=* | fields principal roles
| mvexpand roles
| join principal [| rest /services/iam/principals splunk_server=* | fields principal roles | mvexpand roles | rename roles as other_role]
| where roles!=other_role
| lookup sod_matrix.csv role_a=roles role_b=other_role OUTPUT toxic permitted_with_compensating
| where toxic="true" AND permitted_with_compensating!="true"
| table principal roles other_role
description: Enforces the SoD matrix every day across all principals. Toxic combos become visible the moment they are assigned, not at the next audit.
value: SoD failures are the top SOX ITGC finding in the Fortune-1000 cohort; automating them is the only defensible posture.
implementation: (1) Maintain sod_matrix.csv with the company's SoD rules; (2) ingest IAM state via REST; (3) run daily; (4) on finding, auto-open a remediation ticket.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: See subcategory preamble..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain sod_matrix.csv with the company's SoD rules; (2) ingest IAM state via REST; (3) run daily; (4) on finding, auto-open a remediation ticket.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| rest /services/iam/principals splunk_server=* | fields principal roles
| mvexpand roles
| join principal [| rest /services/iam/principals splunk_server=* | fields principal roles | mvexpand roles | rename roles as other_role]
| where roles!=other_role
| lookup sod_matrix.csv role_a=roles role_b=other_role OUTPUT toxic permitted_with_compensating
| where toxic="true" AND permitted_with_compensating!="true"
| table principal roles other_role
```

Understanding this SPL

**Segregation of duties — toxic role combinations in IAM** — SoD failures are the top SOX ITGC finding in the Fortune-1000 cohort; automating them is the only defensible posture.

Documented **Data sources**: See subcategory preamble. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Calls Splunk `rest` to read configuration or REST-exposed entities.
• Keeps or drops fields with `fields` to shape columns and size.
• Expands multivalue fields with `mvexpand` — use `limit=` to cap row explosion.
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• Filters the current rows with `where roles!=other_role` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where toxic="true" AND permitted_with_compensating!="true"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Segregation of duties — toxic role combinations in IAM**): table principal roles other_role


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table of violations, network graph of role interactions, single-value 'principals with SoD violations'.
visualization: Table of violations, network graph of role interactions, single-value 'principals with SoD violations'.
knownFalsePositives: Break-glass admin accounts require explicit permitted_with_compensating=true.
